ontology.foodex2_description: Burrito — wheat-flour tortilla filled with beans, rice, and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated vegetarian burrito code; A0FKP (burrito) used as closest parent analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for vegetarian burrito identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard vegetarian burrito weighing approximately 220 g, consisting of a wheat-flour tortilla filled with rice, beans, avocado, salsa, cheese, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Burrito, with beans, cheese, sour cream, and vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21340 / FDC ID 173957)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record values sourced from Singapore HPB database (cr_id G00010824, confidence 0.95). USDA FDC SR Legacy entry for bean-and-vegetable burrito used as cross-reference anchor. HPB values are retained as primary because the input provenance is canonical and confidence is 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g/100g based on USDA FDC composition for comparable bean-cheese-sour-cream burrito; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetarian burrito found in University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 estimated from analogous mixed bean-and-rice dish (GI ~50–55 for bean-rice combinations per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Beans lower the overall GI of the rice-tortilla base; flour tortilla GI ~30 (University of Sydney GI Database, tortilla entry); combined dish estimated at GI 52 (low). GL per serving calculated as: (52 × 20.06 g carbohydrate per 100g × 220 g serving) / 100 = 22.95, rounded to 23.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg/100g with low potassium offset (126 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: At 470 mg sodium per 100 g and only 126 mg potassium per 100 g, this burrito provides an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a burrito and request low-sodium preparation with reduced cheese and no added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 20.06 g/100g with low dietary fibre 1.4 g/100g elevating glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of white rice and wheat-flour tortilla delivers 20 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.4 g fibre, producing a glycaemic load of approximately 23 per standard serving; people with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice and increase bean proportion to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 212 mg/100g and estimated saturated fat 4.5 g/100g from cheese and sour cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cholesterol at 212 mg per 100 g and estimated saturated fat of 4.5 g per 100 g from cheese and sour cream are clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management; individuals with CVD should reduce or omit cheese and sour cream, increase avocado for MUFA, and limit portion to 100–120 g to keep saturated fat intake within daily targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA from avocado; nightshade vegetables may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Avocado and beans provide anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fat and fibre; however, bell peppers, tomato salsa, and chilli are nightshades containing solanine, which may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals — monitor personal tolerance and reduce nightshade ingredients if flares occur.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A vegetarian burrito is a Mexican-American wrapped dish consisting of a large wheat-flour tortilla filled with cooked rice, seasoned beans (typically black or pinto), guacamole or sliced avocado, fresh salsa, sautéed bell peppers, shredded cheese, and sour cream. The tortilla is folded and rolled to enclose the filling, creating a portable, hand-held meal. The texture combines the soft chew of the flour tortilla with the creamy richness of avocado and the hearty bite of beans and rice. The flavour profile is savoury with mild heat from salsa and a slight tang from sour cream. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 249 kcal, 10.91 g protein, 13.71 g fat, and 20.06 g carbohydrate with only 1.4 g dietary fibre, making it moderately energy-dense. Sodium is elevated at 470 mg/100 g, which is a concern for hypertension management. Cholesterol is high at 212 mg/100 g due to cheese and sour cream content. The estimated glycaemic index is 52 (low), moderated by beans, but the glycaemic load per standard 220 g serving is approximately 23, which is clinically significant for type 2 diabetes. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to dairy. It is suitable for halal diets. For cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat and cholesterol content warrant portion control. Avocado contributes beneficial monounsaturated fatty acids and potassium. This dish is commonly consumed as lunch or dinner in Western and Singaporean food-court contexts.
common_user_queries: Is vegetarian burrito high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB canonical record (confidence 0.95, provenance canonical). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FDC analogous bean-cheese-sour-cream burrito entry as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed data for bean-rice combinations. Detailed micronutrient profile (vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, water) not available in HPB source and set to -1.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FDC SR Legacy entry for bean-and-vegetable burrito (FDC ID 173957) as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data for this specific vegetarian burrito preparation not available in the Singapore HPB database or USDA FDC as of 2026-04-16. 3. The input record name 'Vegetabian Burritos' appears to be a typographical variant of 'Vegetarian Burritos'; canonical English name corrected to 'Vegetarian Burrito'. The original alias 'Vegetabian Burritos' is preserved in the aliases array. 4. Cholesterol at 212 mg/100g is notably high for a vegetarian dish and is attributed to cheese and sour cream content; this value is preserved from the HPB canonical source. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. This dish is classified as Western/Mexican-American cuisine; Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply as this is not a Singaporean dish.
